author: potuz
content:
I think we want both and in different form. We want a beacon API endpoint to submit them ahead of time and the beacon simply forwards them to the builder via a builder API endpoint. And we want this one jit. 

However, the preferences are different in each case and should have different semantics so the naming needs to be fixed. I don't want to deal with either API. But the point is that the preferences that are forwarded to the builder do not need to be the same than the preferences that reach the beacon. The former are the ones that the VC wants to be exposed. The latter are all that the BN needs to make a choice of a bid. They do not need to be the same set and in fact I suspect the VC wants to expose just a minimum set like "yes I will take trusted bids from you" and nothing more

author: nflaig
content:
> "yes I will take trusted bids from you"
I hope we can use `max_execution_payment` for that, ie. `max_execution_payment=0` meaning only trustless, that's what I discussed with Bharath, not sure if this is documented, I think I saw it in his builder spec pr though

author: yorickdowne
content: This looks good. Thanks for putting that together!

author: serenita_io
content:
I still haven't heard a good reason why we're spending so much time on *this amount of per-key configurability*. I foresee the vast majority of node operators will be using very simple configurations (see my PR comment). Do we have any reason to believe otherwise? Before anyone says Lido CSM/RP, I don't think running 2 VC instances is such a heavy burden for node operators if it avoids a ton of client-code complexity.

You could argue - why not do it anyway? It is easy to implement with AI nowadays. But that is not my point. Even with AI, it still adds hundreds of lines of code across multiple codebases that may end up being completely unused.

To drive my point further, say I'm a node operator and know an up-and-coming APAC builder that I want to support. I want to prefer their bids during Japanese daytime (say 0-9 UTC). Should we add a dedicated config option for that? Of course not, because I'd probably be the only one who would use it.

-> Are these per-key per-builder tweaks truly necessary?

author: potuz
content: This is one of the examples in the issue I think. But it's setting this to zero or max uint

author: potuz
content: It is absolutely necessary! A rational operator will connect directly to **anyone** that has an endpoint. And definitely will want to not take trusted bids from anyone. Just this is enough to have at least a binary list of builders. Second: a rational operator will prefer to send signed auths per builder anyway, therefore the endpoint anyway has to receive already a list of builders with different entries. Filling a list with a default value for other entries is trivial so no extra complexity to clients

author: potuz
content: A client that doesn't want to implement or expose this granularity to users is fine. The API does not need to leak into the CLI

author: serenita_io
content:
I kind of understand the per-builder configuration argument and why you'd perhaps want to make that configurable. But a per-key granularity for this seems excessive, reiterating I have not seen anyone ask for that.

Have you considered the approach of the validator client selecting a bid directly, instead of passing all this data to the attached beacon node(s)? I want to explore if that approach is feasible since it could significantly simplify (and even remove the need for some) Beacon API interactions. When the time to produce a block comes, the VC would select a bid by itself and pass it to the beacon node in the produceBlock call. The beacon node should still be able to override the VC bid e.g. in case of circuit breaker triggering or something similar.

author: potuz
content:
The per-key is kinda irrelevant for this API isn't it? the API only gets the data for a single Key which is the key that is proposing, so the only thing that matters is the per-builder part. 

How clients allow operators to configure their keys, if all with the same parameters or allowing different parameters should be kinda irrelevant for this API and transparent. Having said so, I am a very small operator that has different fee recipients among my unconsolidated genesis validator and my consolidated ones. Add to this a different configuration for CSM and suddenly I'd need at least three different VCs connected to my node if Prysm didn't allow me to configure this per-key.

author: potuz
content: I can easily see how others may be in a similar tax situation as myself and require different fee recipients/withdrawal addresses for different validators.

author: serenita_io
content:
Yes, per-key is mostly irrelevant for the Beacon API but very relevant to the linked Keymanager API discussion.

I agree it is useful to configure some things on a per-key level, like the fee recipient or graffiti. I still don't quite see how actually useful it is to set varying per-builder options on a per-key level.

But my second point above is relevant to the Beacon API design - if the validator client becomes mostly responsible for bid selection then it no longer needs to pass bid-selection-relevant data to the beacon node.

author: potuz
content: The beacon is the one that selects the bid not the validator client. The beacon needs this info from the VC to decide

author: serenita_io
content: Is there a reason why the VC couldn't do the bid selection?

author: serenita_io
content: (while allowing the beacon node to override)

author: potuz
content: That's a pretty invasive change: suddenly the VC will need to understand and parse data, need to know how to verify the signature and the bid itself etc

author: potuz
content: The vc is a pretty simple and contained piece of software

author: serenita_io
content: It is, though it doesn't seem that far-fetched to me if it simplifies the Beacon API interactions in a major way

author: potuz
content: Also with security consequences: the VC can be isolated now. But with your proposal it will have to connect directly to builders in the wan
